claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 3 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 2 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 3 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 3 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me start by asking you some basic questions to determine your eligibility.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for:**

How old is your child? 

Please let me know:
- Are they **under 16 years old**?
- Are they **16 to 19 years old**?
- Or are they **20 or older**?

This will help me determine the next steps in assessing your eligibility. If you have multiple children, let's start with one child at a time and work through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh hello, thanks for helping me with this. I'm actually inquiring about my three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. They're all quite young - Alex is 3, Blake is 2, and Charlie is also 3. So yes, they're all under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'm happy to help you with Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Since all three children (Alex age 3, Blake age 2, and Charlie age 3) are under 16, they meet the basic age requirement. Now let me check the next criteria.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, the next question is about whether the children are in paid work:

**Are any of your children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) working 24 hours or more per week in paid work?**

Given their young ages (2 and 3 years old), I assume the answer is no, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - they're only 2 and 3 years old! None of them are working at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now the next question:

**Are any of your children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) receiving benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?**

Again, given their very young ages, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm this as part of the eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them are receiving any benefits in their own right. They're just toddlers.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that makes sense. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Continuing with the assessment:

**Are any of your children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) in an apprenticeship program?**

Given that they're only 2 and 3 years old, I'm confident they're not, but I need to ask as part of the formal assessment process.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - they're far too young for any apprenticeships!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You'll be eligible if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family (and not claiming certain benefits like Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit)
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Which of these applies to your situation?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK - I live in Manchester with my family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That satisfies the residency requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to confirm your relationship and responsibility for the children.

**Are you responsible for Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

You're considered responsible if ANY of the following applies:

1. **You live with the children**
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care - including food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

**Do you live with Alex, Blake, and Charlie, or do any of the other situations apply?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, it's a bit complicated actually. Blake lives here at home with me in Manchester. But Alex and Charlie don't live with me - they live with their other parent. Though I do help out with Alex financially - I contribute towards clothes, school things, that sort of thing.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining the situation. Let me make sure I understand correctly:

- **Blake (age 2)**: Lives with you in Manchester ✓
- **Alex (age 3)**: Lives with their other parent, but you contribute financially (clothes, school things, etc.)
- **Charlie (age 3)**: Lives with their other parent

For **Blake**, since they live with you, you meet the responsibility requirement.

For **Alex**, since you contribute financially towards their upkeep (clothes, school things), you may meet the responsibility requirement - but I need to clarify: Do your contributions to Alex's care equal or exceed the Child Benefit amount? 

Let me get the exact amounts for you:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The current Child Benefit rates aren't specified in my system, but as of the latest rates, it's approximately £25.60 per week for the eldest child and £16.95 per week for additional children. 

**For Alex**: Do your financial contributions (clothes, school things, etc.) amount to at least around £16-17 per week (or about £70-75 per month)?

**For Charlie**: Do you contribute anything financially towards Charlie's upkeep, or have any other form of responsibility for them?

This will help me determine which children you can claim Child Benefit for.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex, yes - I contribute about £26 a week towards their upkeep, so that should cover it.

For Charlie, no, I don't contribute anything financially. Their other parent handles everything for Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying! So to summarize:

- **Blake (age 2)**: Lives with you ✓ - You're responsible
- **Alex (age 3)**: You contribute £26/week towards their upkeep ✓ - You're responsible  
- **Charlie (age 3)**: No contribution, other parent handles everything ✗ - You're NOT responsible

Let me continue the assessment for Blake and Alex first, as you meet the responsibility requirement for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask an important question about other claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake or Alex?**

This is particularly important since you mentioned Alex lives with their other parent. 

- For **Blake** (who lives with you): Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Blake?
- For **Alex** (who lives with the other parent): Is the other parent claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

If someone else is claiming, we need to know if:
1. There's no other claimant
2. The other claimant has agreed to transfer the claim to you
3. There's a dispute about who should claim

What's the situation for each child?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Blake, no - nobody else is claiming for him. He lives with me and I'm the only one claiming.

For Alex, well, their other parent lives with Alex, and I believe they're already claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. This creates different situations for each child:

**For Blake**: Since nobody else is claiming and he lives with you, we can continue with the eligibility assessment for him.

**For Alex**: Since the other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, this affects your ability to claim. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at any given time.

**Important question about Alex**: Have you and Alex's other parent discussed or agreed that you should claim Child Benefit for Alex instead of them? Or are you hoping to claim for Alex while they continue to claim?

The rules are:
- If the other parent agrees to transfer the claim to you, that's possible (though given Alex lives with them, they would typically be the priority claimant)
- If you both want to claim and there's a dispute, HMRC would need to decide
- Only one person can receive the benefit at a time

**What's your intention regarding Alex's Child Benefit?** Are you:
1. Just trying to understand if you *could* be eligible to claim for Alex (even though the other parent currently claims)?
2. Planning to ask the other parent to transfer the claim to you?
3. Or is there a dispute about who should claim?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I see. No, I'm not trying to take over the claim from them or anything like that. I just wanted to understand my situation and see if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I contribute financially to Alex, so I wasn't sure how it all worked. If they're already claiming for Alex, then that's fine - they live together after all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That makes perfect sense! Thank you for clarifying. So let me continue the assessment focusing on **Blake**, since:

- Blake lives with you and nobody else is claiming for him
- For Alex, the other parent is appropriately claiming since Alex lives with them

Let me proceed with Blake's eligibility:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check Blake's living situation:

**For Blake (age 2), which of the following applies?**

1. **Living normally at home with you** (the usual situation)
2. In hospital
3. In local authority care
4. Fostered

I assume Blake is living normally at home with you in Manchester, but I need to confirm this to complete the assessment properly.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Blake is living normally at home with me here in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Just one final question for Blake:

**Is the local council paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, are you receiving any payments from the local council specifically for Blake's living costs or care? (This would be different from general benefits like Universal Credit or Housing Benefit that you might receive for your household.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the local council isn't paying anything towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Let me finalize the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me provide you with the complete assessment results:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## **Your Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Complete**

### **Summary:**

You **ARE ELIGIBLE** to claim Child Benefit for **Blake**, but not for Alex or Charlie.

---

### **Individual Child Assessments:**

**✅ Blake (age 2) - ELIGIBLE**
- Blake meets all the eligibility criteria
- Blake is under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in an apprenticeship
- You live with Blake in Manchester and are responsible for them
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake
- Blake lives normally at home with you
- The local council is not paying towards Blake's care

**❌ Alex (age 3) - NOT ELIGIBLE for your claim**
- While Alex meets the basic qualifying criteria and you do meet the responsibility requirement (contributing £26/week), Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at any given time
- Since Alex lives with the other parent, they are the appropriate claimant

**❌ Charlie (age 3) - NOT ELIGIBLE for your claim**
- You do not meet the responsibility requirement for Charlie
- You don't live with Charlie and don't contribute financially to their upkeep
- Without meeting the responsibility requirement, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Charlie

---

### **Next Steps for Blake:**

1. **Apply for Child Benefit** - You can apply online or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Blake
3. **Provide Blake's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through your tax return
5. **Payments**: Child Benefit is usually paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

You can apply here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

---

Do you have any questions about this assessment or the next steps for claiming Child Benefit for Blake?

reasonings: Blake is eligible for Child Benefit. Blake is under 16 years old (age 2), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with you in Manchester, you are responsible for them, no other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, Blake lives normally at home with you, and the local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Alex is not eligible for your Child Benefit claim. While Alex meets the basic qualifying child criteria (under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship) and you meet the responsibility requirement by contributing £26/week towards Alex's upkeep, Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time, and since Alex lives with the other parent, they are the appropriate claimant.

Charlie is not eligible for your Child Benefit claim. You do not meet the responsibility requirement for Charlie as you do not live with Charlie and do not contribute financially towards Charlie's upkeep. The other parent handles everything for Charlie. Without meeting the responsibility requirement, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You have three children: Blake (age 2), Alex (age 3), and Charlie (age 3). Blake lives with you in Manchester and you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake as all criteria are met. Alex lives with their other parent who is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, so you cannot claim for Alex (only one person can claim per child). Charlie also lives with the other parent and you do not contribute to Charlie's upkeep, so you do not meet the responsibility requirement and cannot claim for Charlie.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate

Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

All eligibility criteria are met.

Alex is not eligible for your Child Benefit claim. While Alex meets the basic qualifying child criteria (under 16 years old, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship) and you meet the responsibility requirement by contributing £26/week towards Alex's upkeep, Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time, and since Alex lives with the other parent, they are the appropriate claimant.

Charlie is not eligible for your Child Benefit claim. You do not meet the responsibility requirement for Charlie as you do not live with Charlie and do not contribute financially towards Charlie's upkeep. The other parent handles everything for Charlie. Without meeting the responsibility requirement, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Charlie.
